coredata.dc:title: Self-calibrated ratiometric thermometers and multi-mode anti-counterfeiting based on Ca2LaNbO6:Pr3+ optical material
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Anqi
Sun, Zhen
Wang, Zhiying
Jia, Mochen
Choi, Byung Chun
Fu, Zuoling
Jeong, Jung Hyun
Park, Sung Heum
coredata.dc:description: Optical materials have been extensively used in thermometers and anti-countering for their non-contact, advantageous rapid, and high precision properties. Herein, a double-perovskite Ca2LaNbO6:Pr3+ color-tunable phosphor was synthesized. The emissions (blue, green, red1, and red2) from Ca2LaNbO6:Pr3+ phosphor have different temperature-dependent properties because of the thermal quenching channel generated by the intervalence charge transfer state between Pr3+ and Nb5+ ions. They can be used to realize four fluorescence intensity ratio models (Green/Blue, Green/Red2, Red1/Blue and Red1/Red2) to measure temperature with high absolute and relative sensitivities. Moreover, using the Ca2LaNbO6:Pr3+ phosphor and the carbon dots with green afterglow to create anti-counterfeiting patterns, the multi-mode anti-counterfeiting under different portable lamps (254 nm and 365 nm) was realized. Therefore, the prepared multifunctional phosphor indicated a potential application in both optical temperature sensing and anti-counterfeiting simultaneously.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pr3+ ions
Down-conversion luminescence
Fluorescence intensity ratio
Temperature sensing
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Nano·Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by Ministry of Science and ICT (grant number- 2021M3H4A1A02057007 ), the Research Grant of Pukyong National University in 2021, Functional Phosphor Bank at Pukyong National University ( 2017M3A9B8069470 ). This research was also supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11874182 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of Ca2LaNbO6:x%Pr3+ (x = 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9) samples; Rietveld refinement XRD patterns of the representative (b) CLNO host and (c) CLNO:3%Pr3+ using GSAS software; (d) structure of the Ca2LaNbO6 crystal; (e) SEM image of the Ca2LaNbO6:Pr3+ phosphor. (f–j) Elemental mapping of Ca, La, Nb, O and Pr, respectively.

(a) energy level diagram of Pr3+ ions and the PLE/PL spectra of the CLNO:Pr3+ phosphor; (b) Energy transfer diagram of the CR mechanisms; photoluminescence decay curves of the 3P0 and 1D2 levels upon observation at (c) 503 and (d) 624 nm, respectively.

(a) 3D color-filled contour of emission spectra of the CLNO:3%Pr3+ sample under 450 nm excitation at various temperatures; (b) CIE chromaticity diagram of the CLNO:3%Pr3+ sample at various temperatures; (c) integrated emission intensities of blue, green, red1, and red2 emissions. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence intensity ratios of (a) green/blue, (b) green/red2, (c) red1/blue, and (d) red1/red2 varying with 1/T; corresponding absolute (e) and relative sensitivities (f) estimated vs. T of the CLNO:3%Pr3+ sample; (g) schematic configuration coordinate diagram of the increasing-temperature-induced nonradiative relaxation processes of Pr3+; photographs of the “PKNU” pattern, under the excitation of (h) 254 nm UV lamp on, (i) 365 nm UV lamp on, (j) both 254 nm and 365 nm UV lamps on, and (k) UV lamp off. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The calculated maximum Sa and Sr values of CLNO:0.5%Pr3+, CLNO:3%Pr3+ and CLNO:9%Pr3+ samples.
Optical thermometric properties of FIR temperature-sensing materials.

head.ce:title: Self-calibrated ratiometric thermometers and multi-mode anti-counterfeiting based on Ca2LaNbO6:Pr3+ optical material

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Physics, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Materials Science & Technology and Key Laboratory for Microstructural Material Physics of Hebei Province, School of Science, Yanshan University, Qinhuangdao, Hebei 066004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Materials Science & Technology and Key Laboratory for Microstructural Material Physics of Hebei Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bState Key Laboratory of Materials Science & Technology and Key Laboratory for Microstructural Material Physics of Hebei Province, School of Science, Yanshan University, Qinhuangdao, Hebei 066004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Coherent Light and Atomic and Molecular Spectroscopy Laboratory, Key Laboratory of physics and Technology for Advanced Batteries, College of Physics, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Coherent Light and Atomic and Molecular Spectroscopy Laboratory
Key Laboratory of physics and Technology for Advanced Batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cCoherent Light and Atomic and Molecular Spectroscopy Laboratory, Key Laboratory of physics and Technology for Advanced Batteries, College of Physics, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Optical materials have been extensively used in thermometers and anti-countering for their non-contact, advantageous rapid, and high precision properties. Herein, a double-perovskite Ca2LaNbO6:Pr3+ color-tunable phosphor was synthesized. The emissions (blue, green, red1, and red2) from Ca2LaNbO6:Pr3+ phosphor have different temperature-dependent properties because of the thermal quenching channel generated by the intervalence charge transfer state between Pr3+ and Nb5+ ions. They can be used to realize four fluorescence intensity ratio models (Green/Blue, Green/Red2, Red1/Blue and Red1/Red2) to measure temperature with high absolute and relative sensitivities. Moreover, using the Ca2LaNbO6:Pr3+ phosphor and the carbon dots with green afterglow to create anti-counterfeiting patterns, the multi-mode anti-counterfeiting under different portable lamps (254 nm and 365 nm) was realized. Therefore, the prepared multifunctional phosphor indicated a potential application in both optical temperature sensing and anti-counterfeiting simultaneously.

The optical non-contact Ca2LaNbO6:Pr3+ phosphors have potential in self-calibrated temperature sensing with high sensitivity by four FIR models and anti-counterfeiting under different portable UV lamps.

ce:sections.ce:para: The temperature sensor is a vital component in medical treatment, industrial production, and daily life [1–3]. Compared with the conditional physical temperature probes, optical thermometers can be used in non-contact scenarios, hostile environments, small volumes, and dynamic systems [4,5]. Optical thermometers can be realized using various temperature-dependent parameters, such as fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR), decay lifetime, emission peak position, and emission bandwidth [6,7]. FIR technology uses two emission intensities as monitor signals, resulting in its advantages such as high accuracy and high sensitivity [8–10]. Lanthanide ions (Pr3+ or Tb3+) and transition metal ions (Mn+=Ti4+, V5+, Zr4+, Nb5+, Ta5+, Mo6+, W6+) can form the metal–metal intervalence charge transfer (IVCT) state, which interferes with lanthanide ions luminescence by providing a quenching channel; [8,11-13] therefore, optical thermometers with high sensitivities can be designed. In addition, optical anti-counterfeiting fluorescent materials have been developed for information security due to their simple manufacturing processes, long service life, diverse light emission, and low cost, but the traditional phosphors with a single photoluminescence mode are easily imitated [14–17]. Up-conversion phosphors have been developed to realize the second anti-counterfeiting mode [18,19], but the high-power laser might be dangerous. Especially infrared laser with high power is potentially harmful because it is invisible to the human eyes. It is necessary to design a multi-mode optical anti-counterfeiting materials under low-power portable lamps. Optical materials with double perovskite structure have been investigated in many fields such as solar cells, light-emitting diodes, and X-ray imaging owing to their high environmental stability and low toxicity [20–22]. Here, we synthesized the Ca2LaNbO6 (CLNO) phosphor with double perovskite structure as the host owing to the decent thermal stability, and IVCT state between Pr3+ ions and Nb5+ ions [23]. The CLNO:Pr3+ samples exhibit four emissions of characteristic 4f–4f transition of Pr3+ ions under 450 nm excitation: blue (3P0→3H4), green (3P1→3H5), red1 (1D2→3H4), and red2 (3P0→3F2). Self-calibration thermometer through simultaneous monitoring of four FIR models (G/B, G/R2, R1/B, and R1/R2) can be realized. The other three models can be monitored to correct the error of one model. Additionally, CLNO:Pr3+ phosphors emitted visible light under the excitation of 254 nm portable ultraviolet (UV) lamp. Whereas CLNO:Pr3+ phosphors have no photoluminescence under 365 nm UV lamp due to the absence of absorption at 365 nm. The green afterglow carbon dots (CDs) [24], which exhibited blue emission under 365 nm UV lamp and green afterglow after the lamp turned off, can combine with the CLNO:Pr3+ phosphors to realize multi-mode anti-counterfeiting. The green afterglow mainly originates from the surface group on CDs (e.g., C = N, C = O) [25]. The synthesis methods of the CDs and CLNO phosphors are presented in the supplementary material.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Ca2LaNbO6:x%Pr3+ (x = 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9) phosphors are shown in Fig. 1
(a). All diffraction peaks matched closely with the pure phase (JCPDS no. 70–1157) [26]. To further explore the structural variation, we conducted an XRD Rietveld refinement analysis of the CLNO host and produced CLNO:3%Pr sample using the software GASA with the crystallographic information file (ICSD no. 252,169) [ Fig. 1 (b, c)] [27]. The refined data are listed in Table S1. The structure of [Ca1O6] octahedron, [NbO6] octahedron, [Ca2/LaO7] polyhedron, and the unit cell of CLNO are shown in Fig. 1 (d). The Pr3+ ions could replace La3+ ions because they have similar radii (La3+= 1.320 Å and Pr3+= 1.286 Å). Fig. 1 (e) shows the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of the CLNO:Pr3+ sample. The sample was assembled by irregular particles. The Ca, La, Nb, O, and Pr elemental mapping images of the CLNO:Pr3+ sample are shown in Fig. 1 (f–j), indicating that the Pr3+ was successfully doped into the CLNO sample. We also conducted X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of the materials to obtain the chemical environment of the constituent elements (Fig. S1). The signals of Ca 2p, La 3d, Nb 3d, and O 1 s peaks were obtained. Additionally, the 3d5/2 peak of Pr3+ ions is shown in the inset figure. All the analysis results indicated that Pr3+ replaced La3+ in the CLNO lattice.

Fig. 2
(a) shows the photoluminescence excitation (PLE), photoluminescence (PL) spectra, and the energy level diagram of the CLNO:Pr3+ samples. Under 450 nm excitation, the PL spectra exhibited four emission peaks at 492, 534, 624, and 657 nm, assigned to the characteristic 4f−4f transitions 3P0→3H4, 3P1→3H5, 1D2→3H4, and 3P0→3F2 of Pr3+ ions, respectively. The PLE spectra monitored at 492 nm indicated two bands (450 nm and 295 nm), which were attributed to the 3H4→ 3P2 transition of Pr3+ and IVCT state, respectively. In term of diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS), the absorption strength of shoulder broadband between 265−345 nm increased with Pr3+ doping (gray arrow) owing to the IVCT state [Fig. S2(a)]. The IVCT state could not be separately obtained in the DRS owing to the overlap with the niobate host absorption (270−400 nm). Through dividing the diffuse reflectance of CLNO:3%Pr3+ by that of CLNO host, a broadband of IVCT state with the maximum energy of 3.66 eV (295 nm, 33,898 cm−1) was clearly observed [Fig. S2(b)], which consistent with the excitation band of IVCT state [Fig. 2 (a)]. The IVCT energy in Pr3+ doped niobate was estimated using an empirical relationship: [28,29]
(1)
IVCT ( c
m
− 1
) = 58800 − 49800
χ ( N
b
5 +
)
d ( P
r
3 +
− N
b
5 +
)
where χ (Nb5+) is the optical electronegativity (1.85), and d (Pr3+ - Nb5+) is the shortest Pr3+ - Nb5+ bond distance, which was determined to be 3.3567 Å according to the crystal refinement result (Table S1). The estimated energy of IVCT band was calculated to be approximately 31,353 cm−1 (3.95 eV), which was consistent with experimental data (33,898 cm−1, 3.66 eV). The PLE and PL spectra with increasing concentration of Pr3+ ions (x = 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9) doped with CLNO samples are shown in Fig. S3(a, b), and the optimal concentration (3%) was obtained from the integral intensities [Fig. S3(c)]. The intensities of red1 emission decreased dramatically, while the green−red ratio increased as the concentration increased from 0.5% to 9% [Fig. S3(d, e)]; which resulted in the luminescence of the samples changed from red to green [Fig. S4]. The color change was due to cross relaxation (CR) [(1D2→3F3:3H4→1G4) and (1D2→1G4:3H4→3F4)] between two neighboring Pr3+ ions [Fig. 2 (b)]. The lifetime (τ) values of 3P0 and 1D2 excited levels, obtained from Fig. 2 (c, d), were used to prove the occurrence of CR. The decay curves of 3P0 emission (λem ∼ 503 nm) [Fig. 2 (c)] and 1D2 emission (λem ∼ 624 nm) [Fig. 2 (d)] were fitted using the following equation: [30]
(2)
I t
=
I 0
+ A exp
( − t / τ )
where It and I0 are the emission intensities; t, A, and τ are the time, constant, and lifetime, respectively. The corresponding lifetimes are presented in Table S2. The lifetimes of the 3P0 excited level did not change significantly [τ = (8.3 ± 0.2) μs] with the concentration of Pr3+ ions, which confirmed that the emissions from the 3P0 level did not exhibit concentration dependence. The radiative transition rate (AR) of the 3P0 level was estimated to be ∼1.2 × 105 s −1. The lifetimes of the 1D2 excited level decreased significantly from 44.0 to 6.8 μs when the Pr3+ concentration increased from 0.5% to 9.0%. The possible CR mechanisms responsible for this observation were (1D2 → 3F3:3H4 → 1G4) and (1D2 → 3H4:3H4 → 3F4) [Fig. 2 (b)]. The CR efficiency (ηCR) was estimated using the relation
η CR
= 1 −
τ
0
. 5 % Pr
/
τ
x % Pr
[31,32], and the maximum value of ηCR was 84.5% for the CLNO:9%Pr3+ sample.

The 3D color-filled contour map of the temperature-dependent emission spectra of CLNO:3%Pr3+ phosphor from 313 to 573 K is depicted in Fig. 3
(a). Fig. 3 (b) shows the CIE chromaticity coordinates shift from green to red with increasing temperature: (0.2919, 0.4966) at 313 K and (0.4472, 0.4314) at 573 K. The integrated intensities of four emissions in Fig. 3 (c) show the different temperature dependencies: 3P0→3H4 (blue) and 3P0→3F2 (red2) emissions decreased faster than the intensities of 3P1→3H4 (green) and 1D2→3H4 (red1) emissions with increasing temperature. Therefore, four models FIR optical thermometer can be designed by using the emissions with different temperature-dependent properties.

The measured plots of the four FIR models versus 1/T are shown in Fig. 4
(a, b, c, d), which can be well fitted by the following equation [33]:
(3)
F I R ≈ B + C exp (
A T
)
where A, B, C are constant parameters, and T is the absolute temperature. Sa and Sr can be derived and expressed by the following equations [9,34]:
(4)
S a
=
|
∂ F I R
∂ T
|
= C exp
(
A T
)
×
A
T 2
(5)
S r
=
|
1
F I R
|
|
∂ F I R
∂ T
|
× 100 % =
C exp ( A / T )
B + C exp ( A / T )
×
A
T 2
× 100 %

Sa and Sr are shown in Fig. 4 (e, f). The maximum values of Sa and Sr were as high as 0.041 K −1 and 0.89%K −1, respectively. In addition, Figs. S5 and S6 show the FIR, Sa, and Sr of the CLNO:0.5%Pr3+ and CLNO:9%Pr3+ phosphors, respectively. The maximum values of Sa and Sr of four FIR models are listed in Table 1 . The CLNO:Pr3+ thermometer exhibits improved sensitivity compared to previously reported FIR optical thermometers as shown in Table 2 . Although the relative sensitivity is not the highest, the other three models can correct the error that occurs in one model of the CLNO:Pr3+ thermometer, providing a reference for self-calibrated thermometer. After six cycles of heating and cooling, the materials exhibit good stability with demonstrating the repeatability of the thermometer (Fig. S7). These results demonstrated that CLNO:Pr3+ is a potential self-calibrated optical thermometer with excellent performance.

The thermal quenching effect for the 3P0 luminescence was possibly controlled by (1) phonon-assisted CR, (2) multi-phonon relaxation (MPR), and (3) crossover to the IVCT state. First, it is widely accepted that the [3P0, 3H4] → [1G4, 1G4] and [3P0, 3H4] → [1D2, 3H6] CR are expected to be enhanced with an increases in temperature [35]. Second, MPR from 3P0 to 1D2 level is discussed. The modified exponential energy-gap equation of Van Dijk and Schuurmans can be used to estimate the MPR rate (WNR) [36]:
(6)
W
N R
= β exp
[ − α (
Δ
E − 2 ℏ
ν max
) ]
where β=107 s −1 and α=4.5(±1) × 10−3 cm−1; [37,38] ΔE denotes the energy-gap between 3P0 and 1D2 (ΔE ≈ 3500 cm−1) [11,39], and ℏνmax is the highest-vibrational energy in the lattice (∼467 cm−1 for CLNO) [Fig. S8 (a)]. Under these conditions, WNR was calculated to be 96.6 s −1. The temperature-dependent WNR can be described using the following equation:
(7)
W
N R
( T )
=
W
N R
( 0 )
[ 1 +
( exp
ℏ
ν max
k T
)
− 1
]
p
where p (p=ΔE/ℏν max) is the number of phonons involved. WNR increased to approximately 732 s −1 at 573 K, which was still lower than the AR of the 3P0 level (1.2 × 105 s −1), indicating that the contribution of MPR was limited. Third, the crossover from the 3P0 excited level to the IVCT state with the assistance of thermal phonons had an indispensable contribution to the thermal quenching. According to the Struck and Fonger model [40], the temperature behavior of 3P0 emission intensity is described by the following equation:
(8)
I ( T )
I ( 0 )
=
[ 1 + A
exp ( −
Δ
E
kT
)
]
− 1

Fig. S8 (b) shows the 1/IBlue versus 1/T curve. ΔE is the required activation energy from the emitting state (3P0 level) to the quenching state (IVCT state) and is approximately 3501 cm−1 (0.43 eV). A configurational coordinate diagram can be constructed [Fig. 4 (g)]. As the temperature increases, the electron in the 3P0 level can overcome the energy barrier to the IVCT state with the assistance of thermal phonons; subsequently, a part of the electron would be relaxed to the 1D2 level and another part would be relaxed to the 3H4 state. In this study, as the temperature increased, blue and red2 emissions decreased dramatically, while green and red1 emissions decreased gradually. Therefore, as described above, a self-calibrated thermometer with four FIR models can be designed using the different temperature-dependence properties of emissions.

We drew the security pattern “PKNU” with the CLNO:Pr3+ color-tunable phosphors and the CDs as shown in Fig. 4 (h–k). In the patterns, “P and N” were prepared with CLNO:1%Pr3+ red phosphor, while the “K”, and “U” were prepared with CLNO:7%Pr3+ green phosphor and CDs, respectively. The detailed synthesis method, XRD patterns, ultraviolet–visible absorption (UV–vis), and PLE/PL spectra of CDs are provided in supplementary materials [Fig. S9]. Because both the CLNO:Pr3+ phosphors and the CDs can emit visible light under UV irradiation of 254 nm, the entire patterns “PKNU” were observed under irradiation of 254 nm or both 254 and 365 nm as shown in Fig. 4 h and j. However, only blue patterns of “U” for CDs was observed under the 365 nm lamp as shown in Fig. 4 i. Since the CLNO:Pr3+ phosphors have no absorption at 365 nm [Fig. 2 (a)], the irradiation of 365 nm only excites the CDs. In particular, the CDs continuously emits bright green afterglow even after the UV lamp off [see the Fig. 4 (k)]. These images can be easily and rapidly identified with the naked eye under different portable UV lamps and have the potential to be used in multi-mode anti-counterfeiting applications.

In this work, a double-perovskite CLNO:Pr3+ color-tunable phosphor was successfully synthesized. Four FIR models (G/B, G/R2, R1/B, and R1/R2) can be used in a temperature sensor. The maximum Sa and Sr of the CLNO:3%Pr3+ phosphor were as high as 4.1%K −1 and 0.89%K −1, respectively. The prepared phosphors and green afterglow CDs can be used to create anti-counterfeiting patterns and realize multi-mode anti-counterfeiting under the excitation of different portable low-energy UV lamps (254 and 365 nm). In summary, our results provide a guidance for both multifunctional optical non-contact materials of thermometer and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Nano·Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by Ministry of Science and ICT (grant number-2021M3H4A1A02057007), the Research Grant of Pukyong National University in 2021, Functional Phosphor Bank at Pukyong National University (2017M3A9B8069470). This research was also supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11874182).
